metadata.title: Why the Warburg Tumor Lights Up on a PET Scan
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 11. Source chapter: cancer-biology/chapters/15-cancer-metabolism-the-warburg-effect-and-glucose.md (FDG-PET and HIF-1alpha mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, TEAL = glucose uptake / HIF active / tumor; CRIMSON = trapped / blocked / cannot proceed; gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = glucose uptake / GLUT transporters / HIF-1alpha active / accumulation; CRIMSON #BF3339 = FDG trapped / cannot proceed / blocked; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO VHL/clear cell RCC (separate card), NO Warburg carbon-allocation argument (separate card), NO IDH or succinate-driven PHD inhibition, NO discussion of FDG-negative tumors beyond one sentence, NO scanner physics. Mechanism: HIF-1alpha stabilization -> GLUT1/HK2 upregulation -> FDG uptake and irreversible phosphorylation trap. Key case: lymphoma PET scan, two pea-sized nodes consuming glucose at 20x normal rate (illustrative).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: FDG-PET uses a radioactive glucose analog to detect tumors that are invisible on CT. The mechanism: cancer cells trap the tracer irreversibly. This reel explains why.

card.copy: Why the Warburg Tumor Lights Up on a PET Scan
card.sub: HIF-1alpha, GLUT1, and the FDG trap

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow, title card

beats.narration_text: Suspected lymphoma patient. CT scan: two pea-sized lymph nodes — normal size, normal density, indistinguishable from surrounding tissue. PET scan of the same nodes: both blazing hot. Glucose consumption twenty times the surrounding tissue rate.

props.headline: A patient with suspected lymphoma. The scan comes back. Two pea-sized…

beats.scene_description: a side-by-side comparison of CT scan (two unremarkable lymph nodes, identical to surroundings) and PET scan of the same region (two nodes glowing intensely against a quiet background), printed as clinical images clipped to aged newsprint
beats.image_prompt: B02, side-by-side CT scan and PET scan of chest lymph nodes printed on newsprint as clinical clippings, left panel CT showing two small lymph nodes indistinguishable from tissue, right panel PET showing same two nodes as bright glowing spots against dark quiet background, desaturated editorial collage, flat print reproduction, no text overlays
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] CT vs PET: two nodes invisible on CT, blazing on PET

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] CT vs PET: two nodes invisible on CT, blazing on PET

beats.narration_text: PET detects glucose uptake. These two nodes are structurally identical to normal tissue on CT but consume glucose at twenty times the normal rate on PET. The question: why do these cancer cells pull in so much more glucose than neighboring cells?

card.copy: Why do cancer cells consume so much more glucose than their neighbors?
card.sub: normal and tumor lymph nodes look identical on CT

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION on screen

beats.narration_text: Energy demand alone does not explain it. A cell burning glucose for ATP should extract thirty ATP per molecule. These cancer cells extract two — and excrete the rest as lactate. Twenty-times-higher uptake with two ATP yield is not an energy problem. It is a different mechanism.

production_viz.label: 2 ATP vs 30 ATP per glucose
production_viz.mechanic: two vertical bars: left TEAL bar labeled 'cancer cell glycolysis' rising to 2, right INK bar labeled 'complete oxidation' rising to 30; both labeled with their values; the gap reads as the puzzle
production_viz.note: the naive expectation fails here — 20x glucose uptake doesn't match 2 ATP yield

beats.narration_text: The mechanism starts with oxygen sensing. Tumors outgrow their blood supply. Cells deep in the mass are genuinely hypoxic. HIF-1alpha is the protein that detects low oxygen and responds — and its response rewires glucose handling.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section card — THE MECHANISM

beats.narration_text: In a normoxic cell, HIF-1alpha is continuously made and immediately destroyed. Prolyl hydroxylases use oxygen to tag it; VHL recognizes the tag; the proteasome degrades it. Half-life: roughly five minutes. Net functional level: absent.

production_viz.label: normoxia: HIF-1alpha made and immediately destroyed
production_viz.mechanic: a top-to-bottom cascade: HIF-1alpha synthesized -> PHD tags it (O2 consumed) -> VHL binds -> proteasome destroys; each step appears as it is spoken; final state shows HIF-1alpha absent
production_viz.note: TEAL for the active destruction chain; show it as the normal healthy state

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] normoxia cascade: PHD -> VHL -> proteasome -> HIF-1alpha gone

beats.narration_text: When oxygen drops, prolyl hydroxylases stall. HIF-1alpha is made but not tagged. VHL cannot bind. Destruction stops. HIF-1alpha accumulates and translocates to the nucleus — where it functions as a transcription factor.

production_viz.label: hypoxia: PHD stalls, HIF-1alpha escapes destruction
production_viz.mechanic: same cascade structure as B06 but PHD step is crossed out / stalled (CRIMSON X); VHL step absent; HIF-1alpha accumulates and an arrow leads to nucleus; accumulation shown as growing TEAL stack
production_viz.note: CRIMSON for the broken/stalled PHD step; TEAL for HIF-1alpha accumulation as the new active driver

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] hypoxia cascade: PHD stalled, HIF-1alpha piles up -> nucleus

beats.narration_text: In the nucleus, HIF-1alpha drives transcription of GLUT1 and GLUT3 — glucose transporters expressed at five times normal density in hypoxic cancer cells — and HK2, hexokinase 2, the enzyme that phosphorylates glucose the moment it enters the cell.

production_viz.label: HIF-1alpha in nucleus -> GLUT1, GLUT3, HK2 upregulated
production_viz.mechanic: HIF-1alpha box in nucleus with arrows fanning to three target chips: GLUT1 (teal), GLUT3 (teal), HK2 (teal); each chip appears with a count indicator showing upregulation; label '5x normal density' under GLUT chips
production_viz.note: accumulate move: targets appear one by one as each is named in narration

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] HIF-1alpha target genes: GLUT1, GLUT3, HK2

beats.narration_text: FDG — fluorodeoxyglucose — is a glucose analog with a radioactive fluorine substituted for one hydroxyl group. GLUT transporters cannot distinguish it from glucose. They transport it into the cell at the same rate.

production_viz.label: FDG pulled in through GLUT1 transporters
production_viz.mechanic: a cell membrane cross-section; multiple GLUT1 transporter marks along the membrane (teal chips); FDG molecule (labeled, teal) arrives from outside and passes through the transporter into the cell interior; second molecule follows
production_viz.note: the accumulate move begins here — FDG entering and building up inside

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] FDG passes through GLUT1 into the cancer cell

beats.narration_text: Inside the cell, hexokinase 2 phosphorylates FDG to FDG-6-phosphate. This is the trap. Normal glucose-6-phosphate can continue down glycolysis or be exported. FDG-6-phosphate cannot: the phosphate blocks export, and the missing hydroxyl group blocks the next glycolytic enzyme. It accumulates.

production_viz.label: HK2 phosphorylates FDG -> FDG-6-phosphate: trapped
production_viz.mechanic: inside the cell: FDG molecule meets HK2 (teal chip); phosphate added -> FDG-6-phosphate; two crimson X marks appear: one on export pathway (blocked), one on glycolysis continuation (blocked); molecule is shown stuck in place accumulating
production_viz.note: CRIMSON for the two blocked routes; TEAL for HK2 activity; the trapped state is the key visual

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] HK2 trap: FDG-6-phosphate blocked from export and glycolysis

beats.narration_text: FDG-6-phosphate accumulates inside the cell. Fluorine-18 decays, emitting a positron. That positron annihilates with a nearby electron, producing two gamma rays in opposite directions. The scanner detects the coincident signal. The node lights up.

production_viz.label: FDG-6-phosphate accumulates -> radioactive signal
production_viz.mechanic: isotype-style accumulation inside the cancer cell: teal squares fill the cell interior one by one (the accumulate move); then a ring pulses from the cell representing radioactive emission; label 'tumor signal' appears
production_viz.note: manim move: accumulate — this is the reel's signature move, showing the trap filling up

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] FDG-6-phosphate accumulates, signal emits, tumor detected

beats.narration_text: The lymphoma case: one hour post-injection, FDG circulates. The cancer node's GLUT1 density is five times normal — HIF-1alpha driven. FDG floods in. HK2 phosphorylates it to FDG-6-phosphate within seconds. It cannot exit. The node glows. The adjacent normal node, with baseline GLUT1 levels, stays dark.

production_viz.label: cancer node vs normal node: two cells side by side
production_viz.mechanic: two cell outlines side by side; LEFT labeled 'cancer node' (TEAL header): many GLUT1 chips on membrane, FDG flooding in, HK2 active, FDG-6-P accumulating (teal fill builds up), signal ring around cell; RIGHT labeled 'normal node' (INK header): few GLUT1 chips, minimal FDG uptake, cell stays dim
production_viz.note: THE EXAMPLE beat: illustrative numbers (5x GLUT1 density, quoted from chapter); the two-cell comparison is the visual payoff of the whole mechanism

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] EXAMPLE — cancer node vs normal node: accumulation vs quiet

beats.narration_text: Limit: FDG-PET detects a metabolic phenotype, not malignancy itself. Slowly-growing tumors and some prostate cancers are FDG-negative — the scan misses them. Use it for cancers with confirmed Warburg metabolism. For those, HIF-1alpha has generated a biochemical signal — irreversible trapping — that structural imaging cannot see.

card.copy: HIF-1alpha -> GLUT1 + HK2 -> FDG trapped -> tumor lights up.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] RECAP endcard — question -> answer in one line

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] RECAP endcard — question -> answer in one line
